title: Strait of Hormuz De Facto Closure
description: Oil tankers avoiding Hormuz after US-Israel strikes on Iran (Operation Epic Fury, Feb 28). Insurance withdrawal by 7 of 12 P&I clubs (effective midnight London March 5) creating de facto blockade. ~20 million bbl/day flow at risk, 84% of which serves Asian markets. QatarEnergy halted LNG production at Ras Laffan complex after drone attacks on March 2. ASEAN crude and LNG supply chains severely disrupted.

title: Thailand Fuel Price Emergency
description: Thailand Energy Ministry warns of sharp diesel pump hike by March 4. Government suspended petroleum exports on March 1 to protect domestic reserves (61 days). Oil Fuel Fund activated to subsidize prices; emergency energy monitoring centre established. Diesel prices risk exceeding $100/bbl. Trade balance most oil-sensitive in ASEAN (-0.9%/$10bbl). Tanker flows through Hormuz and Red Sea remain critical.

title: Indonesia Fiscal Subsidy Crisis
description: Indonesia's 2026 budget sets ICP at $70/bbl. Brent $98.57 (TradingView Jun 3, +4.3% from Jun 2; third consecutive up session; peaked $110.87 D72 Fortune confirmed; ICP overshoot ~$29/bbl). Jun 3: US struck Qeshm Island; Iran drone hit Kuwait airport (Al Jazeera). Rubio rejects Hormuz-linked deal. Tail 42% (up from 35% on triple escalation). USD/IDR ~16,700 est. (+0.6%); Indonesia 10Y ~7.05% est. (+10bp). BI easing window firmly closed. Tail collapse scenario Brent $125-165 on deal collapse.

title: Singapore Refining Hub Under Pressure
description: Singapore, ASEAN's largest oil refining and trading hub, faces margin compression as crude feedstock costs surge. Trade balance sensitivity at -0.7%/$10bbl. Electricity and transport costs rising. Refining margins may stabilise at higher levels as the remaining refining system runs harder - akin to the early Russia-Ukraine conflict pattern - until demand weakens due to high absolute prices. Australian LNG stocks surging as alternative supply sought.

title: Jun 3: US Strikes Qeshm Island; Iran Hits Kuwait Airport
description: Jun 3: US attacked Iran's Qeshm Island — strategic IRGC naval base near Hormuz (Al Jazeera). Iran retaliated with drone strike on Kuwait International Airport: fuel tanks hit, 1 killed, flights suspended. Iran also targeted Bahrain. Rubio: US will lift sanctions only if Iran gives up enriched uranium — rejecting Hormuz-linked deal. Israel escalates Lebanon attacks despite Jun 1 Hezbollah halt. Brent $98.57 (TradingView Jun 3, +4.3%, third consecutive up); WTI $96.25. Hormuz ~90% closed. Tail 42% (up from 35% on triple escalation day). Ships continue sailing 'dark' through Hormuz.

title: Singapore Transhipment Bottleneck Risk
description: Singapore, Tanjung Pelepas, and Port Klang face congestion risk as Gulf-bound cargo is rerouted. Vessels discharging at intermediate ports (Salalah, Colombo) for feeder redistribution. Container surcharges of $1,500-$4,000/TEU adding cost pressure across ASEAN supply chains, compounded by Hapag-Lloyd war risk surcharges on all cargo to/from Upper Gulf, Arabian Gulf, and Persian Gulf from March 2.

title: Cape of Good Hope Rerouting Surge
description: All major carriers (Maersk, CMA CGM, Hapag-Lloyd, MSC) rerouting via Cape of Good Hope after Suez Canal transit suspended. MSC paused all Middle East bookings. Adds 7-14 days and $200-400/TEU in additional costs per voyage. Houthis formally resumed attacks on Israeli and US-flagged vessels on February 28 in response to Iran strikes, creating dual chokepoint crisis with no short route alternative. Analyst Peter Sand (Xeneta): 'repercussions will shatter hopes of a large-scale return of container shipping to the Red Sea in 2026.'

title: ASEAN LNG Supply Disruption
description: 83% of Hormuz LNG volumes are Asia-bound. Nine LNG carriers simultaneously diverted from Hormuz within 6.5 hours on Feb 28. CRITICAL UPDATE: QatarEnergy halted LNG production at Ras Laffan complex on March 2 after drone attacks - Qatar supplies ~20% of global LNG, with 82% of sales going to Asian buyers. Asian LNG spot prices surged ~39%, European benchmark nearly 50%. Thailand faces 25%+ gas price hike by March 4. US LNG export infrastructure at capacity, limiting alternative supply. Southeast Asia faces acute energy security risk.

title: Strait of Hormuz Chokepoint Disruption
description: Brent $98.57 (TradingView Jun 3, +4.3% from Jun 2; third consecutive up); TTF €49.50 (TradingView Jun 3, +11.2%); JKM $18.81 est. (Platts May 22). Jun 3: US struck Qeshm Island; Iran drone hit Kuwait airport (1 killed). Rubio rejects Hormuz-linked deal — triple escalation. Ships sailing 'dark' through Hormuz. CENTCOM: 94+ vessels turned away cumulative. Mine-clearing ~6 months from signature; Ras Laffan restart not before late Aug 2026. Stress pricing $95-110/bbl; tail $125-165/bbl (42%).

title: Singapore Financial Hub Sanctions Compliance Pressure
description: Singapore-based banks and trading firms face heightened secondary sanctions risk. UN sanctions formally reimposed September 27, 2025 (snapback triggered August 28). US conducted two rounds of new Iran designations in February 2026: February 6 (15 entities, 2 individuals, 14 shadow fleet vessels tied to illicit Iranian petroleum) and February 25 (30+ entities, 12 vessels tied to ballistic missile networks and oil sales). 18 Iranian banks under secondary sanctions. Financial institutions must urgently review counterparty exposure to Iran-linked entities and shipping networks.

title: Thailand Inflation Surge Risk from Oil Price Shock
description: Brent $98.57 (TradingView Jun 3, +4.3% from Jun 2; third consecutive up); TTF €49.50 (TradingView Jun 3, +11.2%); JKM $18.81 est. (Platts May 22); tail 42% (up from 35% on Qeshm strike + Kuwait airport + Rubio rejection). USD/THB ~33.10 est. (+0.9%); Thailand 10Y ~2.25% est. (+7bp). BOT easing off table as oil spike resumes; tail collapse scenario drives Brent $125-165.

title: Malaysia Energy Paradox: Petronas Now Net Importer; June Supply Risk
description: PM Anwar confirmed Petronas is now a net fuel importer — exporting premium crude (~350k bpd production) while importing heavy Gulf crude for domestic refining (~700k bpd need), creating structural Hormuz exposure despite traditional exporter status (The Star, paultan.org, Apr 4). Supply secured Apr-May; June uncertain per Economy Minister (Bernama Apr 6). Government response: RON95 fuel quota cut 300L→200L; civil service WFH from Apr 15; unsubsidised petrol RM3.27/L (+68 sen). Pengerang RAPID complex naphtha/condensate feedstock partly routes via Hormuz — IRGC ship seizures (Apr 22) compound supply risk. Petronas-Australia energy supply deal signed Apr 15 begins strategic diversification.

title: Malaysia Tables Parliamentary Motion Condemning Iran Strikes
description: PM Anwar Ibrahim tabled Dewan Rakyat motion on March 2 condemning US-Israel assault on Iran, calling strikes 'barbaric.' Both government and opposition blocs set aside political differences in solidarity with Iran - the strongest and earliest parliamentary response from any ASEAN nation. Anwar called attacks 'a flagrant violation of international law' and urged unconditional ceasefire. Motion passed with full cross-party support, marking a significant departure from ASEAN's traditionally muted responses to Middle East conflicts.

title: Indonesia Offers Tehran Mediation as Neutral Broker
description: President Prabowo Subianto declared willingness to travel to Tehran to mediate US-Iran conflict on February 28. Iran's Jakarta embassy welcomed the offer but urged Indonesia to firmly condemn US-Israel aggression. However, former Indonesian Ambassador to the US Dino Patti Djalal called the proposal 'highly unrealistic,' questioning whether it was adequately considered before announcement. No party has expressed active support for Indonesia's mediation bid. Indonesia positions itself as neutral broker while avoiding direct condemnation of either side.

title: ASEAN Diplomatic Unity Fractures Over Iran Response
description: Jun 3: US struck Qeshm Island; Iran drone hit Kuwait airport. Rubio rejects Hormuz-linked deal — sanctions only lifted on enriched uranium concession. Israel escalates Lebanon attacks despite Jun 1 Hezbollah halt — Netanyahu faces domestic backlash over Lebanon strategy (Al Jazeera Jun 3). ASEAN states maintain neutrality as deal window collapses. Brent $98.57 (TradingView Jun 3, +4.3%); USD/PHP ~57.60 est. (+0.7%); iTraxx Asia IG ~195bp (+13bp); tail 42% (up from 35% on triple escalation). ASEAN neutrality maintained pending deal resolution — but deal pathway now severely damaged.

title: Singapore Balances US Alliance with Middle East Trade Ties
description: Singapore MFA expresses 'regret' over failed negotiations without condemning either side, cancelled flights to the region, and advised citizens to defer travel. As a US security partner with significant Middle East trade exposure, Singapore's carefully calibrated neutrality reflects the tension between alliance commitments and economic pragmatism. Third round of US-Iran nuclear talks in Geneva ended without a deal on February 26 - just two days before strikes began.

title: US Carrier Gap in South China Sea
description: USS Abraham Lincoln CSG departed South China Sea in January 2026 and is now in CENTCOM/Arabian Sea. IRGC falsely claimed it struck Abraham Lincoln with ballistic missiles on March 1 (Pentagon denied). USS George Washington remains forward-deployed at Yokosuka, Japan, and is in maintenance period - limiting operational availability. China increased patrol tempo with formal 'combat readiness patrols' on Feb 17 and Feb 23-26. GPS spoofing and signal jamming ongoing. 500+ US-Philippines exercises planned for 2026 but carrier-level deterrence degraded.

title: Strait of Malacca Elevated Security Risk
description: Malacca Strait piracy reached a 19-year high in 2025 with 108 incidents (74% increase from 2024), the highest count since 2007 per ReCAAP. Hormuz closure rerouting significantly increases Malacca throughput and piracy exposure. Incidents largely low-severity petty theft; no crew abductions. Historical Hezbollah target list includes Singapore Strait and Malacca vessels. INDOMALPHI trilateral patrols under increased operational pressure; Singapore Navy deployed autonomous unmanned surface vessels.

title: Philippines Defense Modernization & ASEAN Summit Security
description: Record 500+ US-PHL joint engagements confirmed for 2026 (agreed at Mutual Defense Board meeting August 2025). Balikatan 2026 described as 'Super Bowl' of exercises - adding cyber, space, area denial, and anti-ship tactics; US deploying additional Medium-Range Capability missile systems. New OPV deliveries from South Korea. Philippine Navy conducting ASEAN Summit security patrols. Chinese signal jamming and SCS confrontations escalating amid US distraction by Iran crisis.

title: US Strikes Qeshm Island; Iran Hits Kuwait Airport
description: Jun 3: US struck Iran's Qeshm Island — strategic IRGC naval base near Hormuz (Al Jazeera). Iran retaliated: drone strike on Kuwait International Airport (1 killed, flights suspended); missiles toward Bahrain. Rubio: sanctions only lifted if Iran gives up enriched uranium — rejects Hormuz-linked deal. Israel escalates Lebanon attacks despite Jun 1 Hezbollah halt. Netanyahu faces domestic backlash over Lebanon strategy. Escalation level raised to 5 (Severe). Brent $98.57 (+4.3%, third consecutive up); tail 42% (up from 35%). Qeshm strike + airport attack + Rubio rejection = triple escalation day.

title: Iran Cyber Retaliation Threat to ASEAN Financial Hubs
description: Major cybersecurity firms (Sophos, SentinelOne) and government agencies issued advisories warning of heightened Iranian cyber activity targeting financial services, critical infrastructure, energy, and telecom sectors in the US, Israel, and allied nations. Active Iran-linked wiper malware deployments and ransomware against Western financial and energy firms confirmed. Southeast Asian financial hubs not directly targeted yet, but are in scope given alliance relationships. Iran's history includes Operation Ababil-style disabling attacks on financial institutions. Southeast Asia's cybercriminal ecosystem remains a potential proxy vector.

title: Jun 3: US Strikes Qeshm Island; Brent $98.57 Third Consecutive Up
description: Brent $98.57 (TradingView Jun 3, +4.3% from Jun 2; third consecutive up session); TTF €49.50 (TradingView Jun 3, +11.2%); JKM $18.81 est. (Platts May 22 carried). Jun 3: US struck Qeshm Island; Iran drone hit Kuwait airport (1 killed). Rubio rejects Hormuz-linked deal. Israel escalates Lebanon attacks. Ships sailing 'dark' through Hormuz. Mine-clearing ~6 months from deal signature; Ras Laffan restart not before late Aug 2026. Tail 42% (up from 35% on triple escalation day).

title: ASEAN Fertilizer Crisis Threatens Rice Belt Food Security
description: Iran's urea production collapse and Hormuz disruption cut ASEAN access to 33% of global fertilizer trade. All eight major fertilizer types increased in price for two consecutive weeks as of late February 2026 per DTN; Middle East freight surcharges adding $10-20/MT delivered cost for Southeast Asian buyers. Thailand, Vietnam, Philippines face 10-15% fertilizer cost increases threatening rice production costs and food affordability for 600M+ people. Indonesia plans to develop 3M additional hectares of rice land requiring extra 500,000 MT/yr urea. Chinese export controls and Russian sanctions create friction for alternative sourcing.

title: Malaysia-Singapore Petrochemical Hub Faces Feedstock Shortage
description: Malaysia's Pengerang Energy Complex and PETRONAS RAPID complex source naphtha and condensate feedstock partly through Hormuz routes. Malaysia-Singapore petrochemical complexes face 15-25% feedstock cost increases as Iranian methanol and Gulf naphtha supplies are disrupted. Regional refining margins under pressure from simultaneous Chinese product dumping and feedstock shortages. Long-term shift to alternative suppliers (Trinidad, Chile, expanded Chinese methanol) underway. New PEC aromatics complex (1.5 MTPA paraxylene) targeting mid-2026 commissioning - feedstock supply agreements with Chevron, Equinor, PTT, and Mitsui carry Hormuz exposure.

title: Vietnam Plastics Industry Squeezed by Dual Disruption
description: Vietnam's plastics and packaging sector faces simultaneous pressure from Chinese manufacturers flooding ASEAN markets with cheap product AND Gulf feedstock cost inflation from Hormuz closure. Long Son complex (1.65M t/y ethylene) provides partial domestic buffer but naphtha price volatility threatens margins across automotive, electronics, and packaging supply chains. Asian naphtha spot prices rising sharply due to Middle East condensate disruption.
